title: The Tale of Digging Three Ponds on the Tip of a Thorn!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All nine source pages were checked for the conference figures, Vision 2023 projects, DMK-era industrial statistics, external quotations, employment figures, and repeated survey passage. The repetition is present in the source and has been retained; a non-text page-transition fragment and visually clear OCR character and word-boundary errors were corrected only here.
paragraphs: People say, “I dug three ponds on the tip of a thorn; two were ruined, and the third never held water.” Jayalalithaa's Investors Meet has ended in just that fashion, like writing the word “sugar” on paper and rubbing the paper upon one's tongue. Yet, without displaying the least embarrassment, Jayalalithaa has announced that Tamil Nadu secured ₹2.40 lakh crore in investment in just those two days.

Statistics from the Central Government's Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion show that during the past year Prime Minister Narendra Modi travelled to twenty-six countries, and the total foreign direct investment obtained for all the States together through those journeys was only ₹1.89 lakh crore. Yet the miracle of Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, seated in one place, amassing ₹2.40 lakh crore through a two-day conference is truly the tale of a mango falling by magic!

Had Jayalalithaa convened this conference four years ago, all these factories would by now have begun operating and milk and honey would be flowing through Tamil Nadu's streets. I cannot understand why they let that golden opportunity slip away!

Jayalalithaa has declared that the rules for starting industries in Tamil Nadu have been simplified and that permission will be obtained within a single month. Yet at a seminar held there, Dhananjeyan, an industrialist from Ariyalur district, courageously rose and argued with the Minister: “For the past two years, I have submitted the proper documents to the Collector and the officials concerned to start an industry. But I have still not received permission.”
The following are passages from an article about the conference in today's Junior Vikatan:

“Andhra Pradesh and Karnataka competed with one another, offering cheap land and tax concessions, holding meetings in Tamil Nadu, and inviting investors. Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah came all the way to Coimbatore and invited them to establish industries in Karnataka. Consequently, investors from Tamil Nadu invested approximately ₹12,000 crore in the newly established 1,595-acre industrial park at Chamarajanagar in Karnataka. Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister Chandrababu Naidu similarly invited Tamil Nadu investors. Only after all this did sudden enlightenment dawn upon the Tamil Nadu Government.

“Had the rulers, who planned so elaborately for Jayalalithaa's participation, taken steps from the outset to attract investors, it would have made history. With only a few months remaining before the 2016 Assembly election, what will they achieve by holding a conference within such a short interval?

“In Tamil Nadu, there is no opportunity to meet and speak to the Chief Minister about starting an industry. Chief Ministers of other States personally travel to many States and countries to gather investment. Jayalalithaa does not meet even industrialists who come to Chennai wishing to see her.

“Jayalalithaa released the long-term ‘Vision 2023’ plan, saying it would take Tamil Nadu along the path of development. But action towards reaching its goals has stalled. Vision 2023 announced that infrastructure would be created for ₹15 lakh crore. By now, ₹4.09 lakh crore should have been spent, but not even ₹4,000 crore has been allotted. It similarly announced that thermal power stations generating 20,000 megawatts would be established, but that target was not reached.

“The Vision 2023 announcements, triangular six-lane and eight-lane roads linking Chengalpattu-Thoothukudi, Thoothukudi-Coimbatore, and Coimbatore-Chengalpattu for ₹24,000 crore; railway development jointly with the Central Government for ₹1.88 lakh crore; airport expansion for ₹25,000 crore; industrial projects for ₹1,60,985 crore; and new homes for urban slum residents for ₹25,000 crore, have all become a mirage.

“Those who issued page after page of statements claiming monorail was ‘best’ and belittling Metro Rail later claimed ownership of the Metro project. They also said monorail projects would be introduced in Madurai, Coimbatore, and Tiruchi. Every one of them proved a damp squib.

“The Central Government introduced the elevated expressway from Maduravoyal to Chennai Port so that cars could be transported easily from automobile factories to the harbour. But the Tamil Nadu Government brought it to a standstill.”
Junior Vikatan has written all this. What is the rulers' reply?

The Chief Minister has said special concessions will be offered to industries established in the southern districts. During the DMK Government itself, a committee headed by former Supreme Court Justice R. Rathinavel Pandian was formed to promote industrial growth in the southern districts and implement progressive schemes benefiting the people living there. Many initiatives were taken on the basis of its recommendations. Through the strenuous efforts of younger brother Murasoli Maran, then a Union Minister, a major Special Economic Zone was planned at Nanguneri and its foundation-stone ceremony was held. The Government that followed failed to make proper arrangements to implement it.
Let me also explain how this department functioned under the DMK Government.

On 8 November 2006, a Special Task Force chaired by the Chief Minister and comprising representatives of industrial and commercial federations was established to support industrial and commercial development in Tamil Nadu.

In 2008, for the first time in India, a separate policy for micro, small, and medium enterprises was formulated. Its aims included increasing their competitiveness and progressively improving performance, promoting agro-based industries to give farmers additional income, and fixing their annual growth rate above ten percent.

Before preparing the Industries Department's demand for grants for 2009-10, younger brother M.K. Stalin, then Deputy Chief Minister, convened a meeting at the Chennai Secretariat on 13 June 2009 of all associations representing micro, small, medium, and large entrepreneurs in Tamil Nadu, and consulted them on steps needed for the State's industrial development. Similar meetings were subsequently held in Tirunelveli, Coimbatore, Tiruchi, and Erode.

In 2011, the Central Statistical Organisation of the Union Government published its Annual Survey of Industries for 2008-09. It reported that Tamil Nadu had 26,122 factories and therefore ranked first among Indian States in the number of factories. Tamil Nadu also ranked first in the number of factory workers, with 17,74,019 people employed in factories alone. It ranked third in total investment, third in total industrial output, and third among the States in net value added by factories.

The Wall Street Journal, in its issue dated 8 July 2010, strongly praised the Tamil Nadu Government's performance. It said: “Tamil Nadu performs better than other Indian States in providing automobile-manufacturing plants with the land, roads, and electricity they require. It also grants more than twelve Government departmental approvals needed to establish or expand an industry at one place through a single-window system.”

After the DMK Government took office in 2006, it formulated a new industrial policy, promoted industrial development, adopted a transparent and industry-friendly approach towards investors, and upgraded industrial infrastructure to global standards. As a result, over five years investment rose by ₹5,73,765.94 crore, reaching a total of ₹7,65,557.92 crore in December 2010, nearly four times the earlier level. A quiet revolution occurred in industrial growth.

A few months ago, K. Rayer, Vice-Chairman of the Central Coir Board, met reporters in Chennai and said: “The Union Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises plans, through the Central Coir Board and with financial assistance from the Central and State Governments, to establish a coir industrial park in Tamil Nadu with an investment of ₹100 crore. The Central Government is prepared to provide ₹70 crore. The State Government need provide only ₹10 crore. If the Khadi and Village Sarvodaya Sangh provides ₹20 crore, this industrial park can be created on 200 acres at Vazhudareddy village in Villupuram.

“But the Tamil Nadu Government has not yet granted permission. For two months I have tried, unsuccessfully, to meet Rural Industries Minister Mohan about this. One can easily meet the President of India and take a photograph with him, but even as a Central Government official I cannot meet a Tamil Nadu Minister. Coconut husks and fibre worth ₹500 crore are burned and wasted every year in Tamil Nadu.”
Newspapers carried this interview in which a Central Government official accused the Tamil Nadu Government.

Whether or not new industries are begun in our State, profitable factories such as Nokia and Foxconn have fled, leaving workers stranded in the street, while the rulers remain indifferent. They show not a trace of concern for the thousands who lost their jobs. Why, there is still no news that many companies which signed agreements with the Tamil Nadu Government have begun their industries.
The Central Statistical Organisation's 2008-09 Annual Survey of Industries, published in 2011, stated again:

- Tamil Nadu had 26,122 factories and ranked first in India in the number of factories. - Tamil Nadu ranked first in the number of factory workers, with 17,74,019 people employed in factories alone. - Tamil Nadu ranked third in total investment. - Tamil Nadu ranked third in total industrial output. - Tamil Nadu ranked third in net value added by factories.

The BIZCON report published by ASSOCHAM described Tamil Nadu as one of India's three leading States in successfully attracting investment. It noted that Tamil Nadu, India's largest State in automobile production and exports, was also one of the world's ten foremost automobile-manufacturing centres. This became possible because of the DMK Government's integrated policy for major automobile projects.

In a special interview to a newspaper dated 4 February 2014, then Union Minister Sudarsana Natchiappan said: “A massive industrial-development project has been brought to Tamil Nadu by the Union Industries Ministry. The Central Government will provide loans covering up to ninety percent of the required investment. The Tamil Nadu Government has shown no interest in implementing it.” He went on to identify the State Government's lack of concern for industrial growth. I asked then what Chief Minister Jayalalithaa would say in reply. Did the Chief Minister or this department's Minister ever answer?

Under the DMK Government, 4,93,000 women worked in factories. Under the present AIADMK Government, the number is 3,78,000. Thus 1,15,000 women have lost employment. Is the loss of jobs by lakhs of women the sign of industrial development?

Speaking recently about industrial development in Tamil Nadu, Subramanian Swamy himself said: “There is neither industrial prosperity nor economic growth. I understand that Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah has attracted industrialists here to his State. Why did the Tamil Nadu Government make no effort to retain the ₹12,000 crore in industrial investment that went from Tamil Nadu to Karnataka? Because there is severe power shortage and non-cooperation from Government officials at every level, no one from other States comes forward to invest in Tamil Nadu.
“The situation in which Tamil Nadu was expected to become the country's leading State in information technology has changed. The reason is the poor administration of the State Government.”
Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, what use is there in becoming angry with us when he has said this?

At the conference, great emphasis was placed on the American company SunEdison coming forward to invest ₹12,000 crore and the Adani Group announcing an investment of ₹15,000 crore. But today's Nakkheeran reports that a representative of a Chinese company participating in the conference said: “We came forward to invest ₹15,000 crore in the same sector as these two companies. We came carrying crores on a platter to give Tamil Nadu electricity, but Jayalalithaa's Government kicked us away and drove us out.”

When Nakkheeran's reporter asked officials about the agreements, one reportedly said: “During the past four and a half years, agreements worth ₹60,620 crore were signed, but only ₹43,000 crore in investment could be obtained. Will the present announcement of ₹2.42 lakh crore in investment end up like a Rule 110 statement read by the Chief Minister in the Assembly?”
When I read reports like these, I am reminded once more of the proverb with which I began: “I dug three ponds on the tip of a thorn; two were ruined, and the third never held water.”
With affection,
M.K.
13 September 2015
